speaker: interviewer
text: The date is November 14, 1996. The survivor is George Steinberg. The interviewer is Donna Kenny. And Longmeadow, Mass is the location.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Steinberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about some Ukrainians who came to your house and told you had to leave your land.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the name of the chief Ukrainian who came and told you that.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] I'll give you a one name. [INAUDIBLE]. You know it's-- especially this. Otherwise, when-- anyway, it's no use to-- I will answer your question, where you were. The first name, I'll give you-- Zachre, Z-A-C-H-R-E. Pcholuk, P-C-H-O-L-U-K-- the second name. It came to my mind, one.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you--

speaker: interviewee
text: A matter of fact, he was-- maybe I'll mention-- the name-- the name what I had a [? farm ?] was also Pcholuk, but not from him. He was even not related, but had the same name. But it doesn't matter. Now this, you asked me-- you asked me the other name. And this is the name [? we're talking about ?] is one more. The other one is Reck R-E-C-K. And the second name was Sweki, S-W-E-K-I-- something like that. It's not exactly the spelling. What I spelled them-- how it came to my mind now on this-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in the-- who then, went into the ghetto with you? Who in your family went to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my family? My wife. My son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is-- now? First was Zelig. I mean, the real name in Jewish, he was called Zelig. He was named after his grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife and son went into the ghetto with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other relatives in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto? Some of them were-- but maybe I don't forget even their names. And from my family, like I mentioned already, they had a mill and good protection from neighbors where they liked them. And they helped them. And they said to the Germans-- they signed with help because they were nice people. Not because they were my-- and they left them in the mill, to work in the mill, and not to take them to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was left to work in the mill?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the mill was two-- three brothers-in-law and-- and my wife's sister. And a cousin. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to talk a little bit about the ghetto itself. Was there a wall around the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wire. Fence with wires.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no way to get out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was two doors. You know, to go in on one side. You can't go out. Two doors, one side, one on the other side. That's only. And by the doors from the ghetto, I call the doors, was on this side, Jewish Polizei they made from the ghetto, picked up some. And the other was Ukrainian police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other Jews in the ghetto besides Jews from Linow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Besides me there were a couple thousand Jews there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all from Linow?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Linow and more [INAUDIBLE] and from the villages. Some Jewish were still living in villages. Had a little store, accustomed from Jewish life in that time in that area. You know, yes. And they all came to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your wife and son and you live in a house in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In a house in a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived in how many rooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: One room. Not only me. Even bigger family. I was a small family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other people living with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't remember exactly. Was four or five families in one house. And the house was not up and down like over here, the buildings. Houses like this. Yes. The size, I would say. Not exactly decorated or something like this, but the size of the houses was this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you cook in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you cook in the-- if we had-- not everybody had what to cook, too. If somebody had, they decide to get outside or inside, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You would cook inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food? Everybody had to look his way to find out to where food-- to find food. Because if a human is hungry, he does everything, what he can and what it came to-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain how you found food for your family in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: To my family? I just mentioned before. But if you ask me, I will answer you again. I have a friend, like I said, this friend that I mentioned to you, the one with a truck. He bring me an-- yes. Every Friday, he used to came to the-- because he was-- he used to bring me food.

speaker: interviewer
text: He would come to the ghetto every Friday to bring you food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And another thing, to tell you my luck. Maybe I mentioned that I was-- they called at the time, Judenrat-- I was the head from my population for my little city. I was the head, and another Jewish people. My name wasn't the same-- Steinberg and his name was Sherman. We were both the heads from our little city. When they put it [INAUDIBLE]. Yeah. And I had the bescheinigung, called in German-- what they call this in English-- document. Because my mind doesn't work the same, like even two hours before.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did this document allow you to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Allowed me-- with a stamp and Judenrat, an officer from this. And I could go out from the ghetto, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whenever I have to. Matter of fact, I used to-- we had-- they called befiehl. We had orders-- will make my language-- orders from the German officers. They want something which in our little city was a luxury when we were in normal life. And they asked something, and if you couldn't find, you have to fill out the-- the orders, what they gave. I went to another city, a bigger city to find, even to collect some and buy there, the things. And this was my job, too, because I had a Judenrat and had a bescheinigung, this-- like a document. I could go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of things would you go and search for to bring back for the German officers?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the German? I can mention a lot of things, and a lot things is even not-- like in our city, the city was no toilets. Toilets.

speaker: interviewer
text: The city was not--

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a toilet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, toilets.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was not. This was not the custom from decent life. What they want, they think, then I had to go and look in a big city to find somewhere, and buy it, and to deliver it to them. Even this things, [? I told you ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to go to the big city and find toilets to be delivered to the officers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the big city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dubno. This was. Like over here, this they call in German Kreis Dubno. Like over here is the capital city for around the little cities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have toilets in the house that you and your family were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was not the custom.

speaker: interviewer
text: You would go outside the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there some place everybody went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Made from wood, from the something. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me the name of the man who would come on Fridays to the ghetto, the man who was the mayor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I could give you the name. Ivan. And the second name-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] this slips from my mind. Waretchuk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-A-R-E-K. Waretchuk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was a Gentile mayor who had been a friend of yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he continued to come and visit you every week to bring food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fine people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his wife, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: The best.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sasha. The best because she gave me, always, even at night, when I was in the underground and the area was [INAUDIBLE] more Jews in [INAUDIBLE] something. I took my chance there at night in a bed, [INAUDIBLE] or something. They came, and they gave me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could your wife and son go with you out of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was not-- this is from the ghetto, you're talking?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the ghetto, and you would go out to get things for the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She could go out the way, with here, if she has a permit. But of course, we had yellow, you know, stripes over here and in the back. Every human, every Jewish person.

speaker: interviewer
text: All Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a different. I had a Star of David with a white band around. This was the-- the thing was-- they know already because, Judenrat. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your wife ever go with you on any of those trips?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She had a child because he was a little boy there, my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do any particular kind of work inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My work was picked up in the morning and they called us in to Germans or to the Polish family because they had the outside offices, which we were connected for certain things. They took slaves. Every day, they give you another number of slaves, how many you need, people to do their work where they needed. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if they needed slaves-- if the Germans needed slaves to work that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. If they need, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: They went to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To me or to another one like me. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Mr. Sherman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because-- yes. Sherman was, and then we had-- because we were from our little city, two officers. They called him off-- and three officers from the city where they brought it in.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you choose who is going to do an assignment and do work for the Germans? How would you pick the people to do that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only with mine. And like anybody else, the people [? when ?] you picked them, they weren't my friend because they are mine. But they are not [? certain. ?] The majority knows this is not my fault or something. I have to fill out the-- yes. There's [INAUDIBLE]. After the war, and you don't ask me this, and maybe I shouldn't even bring it in-- there was ashamed to mention some from other ghettos, Jewish people where they were officers like in my ghetto. Because at that time, human nature, somebody maybe didn't behave. But ours was a little city, and our nature was different also.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in some ghettos, to be in the Judenrat meant that people would be resentful of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A little bit different, yeah. But I'm not talking for a them, I'm talking for myself because I didn't left after the-- on this subject, not because I'm saying over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your ghetto, you never experienced resentment because of what you were asked to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not only me. We were five. Three, like I mentioned from the first-- and two of us. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Before you went into the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and you were still on the farm--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did your wife have help with your baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My wife was in the house. And somehow I don't want to mention, even-- when you-- even between the neighbors, she was maybe more a little bit respectful when they asked for some ladies and this type. More respectful because we had a decent life, and not because my wife is not here and I'm saying-- different-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a girl come live and help your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was not from the Germans. This was when the Russians came in after the Poles. Yes. We had for-- because he was a little kid. And I was a little bit more financially situated. I had a baby girl. She helped for the baby. My wife at that time, we still were from the-- from the better times a couple of months before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the girl who came?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was-- her father was a shoemaker. And the name was Shifra. And the second name was Schwartz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were you able to rescue any of your valuables? Were you able to take any of the important papers or important jewelry or important things with you to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not with permit. You were stealing for yourself if you had a mind and you had something, valuable things. Not with permit-- nothing. With permit, nothing. Nothing from the house. Nothing. Everything we belong was not to you. Didn't belong to you, like to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you all, in the ghetto, did you know what was happening outside in the rest of Poland and Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of it. A lot of histories we know, and not because I'm saying now, but this is-- I tell you, we thought, in that time, in our little ghetto, maybe they will avoid us a little bit longer because we filled out every befiehl, they call in German, the orders, an order. And the officers-- a couple were around the city, German- were satisfied. We thought so. And that's why they will keep us longer. Matter of fact, there was rumors by the name-- he was an officer. And he was good, a German. The name was Schneider. And he left for a month for vacation to his home. He was not from the city, an officer from the army, from the SS. But he had a human feeling, more than the other ones. I call [INAUDIBLE] judgment. And he left for a month for a vacation, like what we call here vacation, to his home. We were afraid, as now they would come and kill us because he was the one that we thought, in our minds, not only in my mind. And he is the one that-- he keeps us alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the people in the ghetto know the truth about the extermination and gas chambers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Of course. This was the scare, and this was the way of thinking, and this was life-- as no hope for tomorrow to be alive because they are killed there and killed there when they took them away from our city. Oh, yeah. Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- you always have some connections. In the worst time, even in war time or something, in any worse time, yes. Yes. Of course. And like I will tell you, it came to mind. You don't ask me. Maybe it's not necessary. When I went for to pick up something in a bigger city, the city was Dubno. And I went there, and I came to the city. It was already, they called in that time, in their language, Aktion. Aktion means they kill Jews. And I left the horse and wagon and went. I was walking home because I find out in the beginning from the city. And this is not stories. This was the life, this happened in life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were in the city--

speaker: interviewee
text: I find-- when I came to the city to pick up something, to find somebody, I came on the beginning. There's something-- and find the Jews. They called it at the time, razia. Then I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got out of the city fast.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any people killed in the Aktion?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see nothing because my eyes, in that time, even didn't look right. Scared dead. This is not a good, you know, way of thinking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever plan to escape the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Like I told you, and I want to also mention-- but it's necessary to mention the name. I was always talking, at that time, not like today, in my life and in my mind. And I was a little bit organized. I thought at the end is waiting. Bad. And I was also-- I don't want to take the credit, between myself and other people, we're going to go out in the woods to be a partisan. You know, like the partisan came after. To go to the wood. And they asked me, you have a wife, the other young-- I was young at the time. And I was always, I don't want to mention name, I take the credit-- aggressive in my life and the way I was at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they asked me, you have a wife and kid? Then I had to answer, what I will do if I take my wife with me? Would be better? I'm not doing this for pleasure. But in the end, they came and took my wife with my kid, because I was working two sides. I find a place, also, in case when it came, I always prepared. I had a decent life, in my life for myself, to survive and to support myself. So you were most concerned about the safety of your wife and your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Always. And the last day, I took my wife and my child. And because the ghetto was closed. They didn't let out. Only because I was a Judenrat-- and I brought them to the door. And because I was a Judenrat with the protection from the inside, they let me out. And they came out. And they took it out right away, the stars, my wife and him. And they went out from the ghetto. And I talked to him, this was our discussion-- as, in case I will be lucky to came before night, to find them there and there by a river between-- in a big, you know, hole. And we will find each other, in case we will be lucky to find each other. And that's what it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was-- excuse me. You want to ask me something else? Maybe I talk too much. And what came to my mind, the reason, I am the subject for you talking to me. And I'd like to explain the way. And I came there, and I find them. And I took my child on the-- on the time. And I went at night, and if you look-- we were wandering, but I couldn't tell you exactly-- two or three months in the woods, in the field-- which was already killed. And then-- it's a long story, every detail is impossible. And when you talk, came to your mind, and you want to put it in even if it's not belong to the subject to talk. But I mention.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you escape the ghetto? Do you remember what season it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: The season, of course. Fall. This was in fall. In our holidays, the Jewish High Holidays. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you leave that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: That night? Because you asked me, and I have to [INAUDIBLE]. Even that time, and God gave me a little bit common sense, and I had already connections with a Polish guard. He worked for the officer in the office. And I had connections with him. And I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was Boboski. And I went to him, and he told me, it's bad. It's bad. Why? Because a big officer from the German SS from Kreis Dubno-- Dubno was the Kreis, like we're the capital from this area, this state.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to dig holes-- he didn't say it's for your people-- I'm talking to you, exactly what he told me. But we think it's for winter, for putting potatoes. The custom from farmers was in Europe at that time from where I came, for winter they used to-- potatoes were in the field, and covered up with dirt, with their way. But that's the way, what we are thinking. But he didn't tell us for this. But we know because we were-- the feeling in the area in the time was [? arrive ?] to wait for bad things. And the feeling from us-- and I came right over [INAUDIBLE], we came on orders [? for us. ?] And I came back to the ghetto. And at that time, I took my wife with my kid and the other-- if you ask me, it came to this subject-- the other, from my little city, the Judenrat, Shlomo Sherman-- you have the name-- came to me and says, George. At that time, they didn't call me George. But I say George. We have a befiehl. Befiehl means, like, order from a big officer from the SS to give him a big forecoat for winter. They didn't call it a coat, but I say a coat. And fore because they are this-- to cover up when they go on a sled in wintertime. And I was nervous already because I just came in. And his name was Shlomo. I mentioned the name there. And I told him, Shlomo, I'm not going anymore to do this. It's danger from us, from nervousness. And not the mind was, in the that time, you can't even think, not in normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the answer was-- he was, what do you say? He said, a panic-- there will be a panic in the-- I say, that's what it is. And that's all. And he went out. And what he told me in that time, I was one of the only Jews who survived. And this is something.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] [SOBBING] I mention the name, not because I wanted-- what I chose at that time, as he's a better Jew with a better feeling. He [INAUDIBLE] survived. That's what the answer to me was. I was one of the only Jews to survive. It was, well, we were told the Jews were with me. That's the answer. And that's why. I was with a different mind, at that time, and in my situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see Shlomo Sherman again?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was killed. Took him out from the ghetto with all his family. The name is Sherman. I have his brothers over here and pictures, some of him. They came from Israel because-- because from his family, survived two brothers' kids. And they're in Israel, young kids. Somehow they survived. And I show you a book that I have from the ghetto from Israel. They made a book of pictures they took. Their pictures are there, too. And they were from the leaders to make the book in Israel now. What it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you tell me again, please, the name of the SS informer, the man you spoke to who warned you about the holes being dug?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't understand you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was his name Boboski?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boboski was a Pollack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a worker for Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Polish guy. I know him. But a good human. Don't think only was good Jews. Was other people, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name? Boboski, B-O-B-O-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first name was, if I'm not mistaken, Edward-- if I'm correct because this is the way which rings in my-- my head. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the time that you were in the ghetto, before you escaped with your wife and son, did you see people killed in your ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was buried-- I was burying some people. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You buried some people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had to go. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there round-ups of people, Aktions of people in your ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: In your ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lots of times. Even at night. Most of night, they'd make-- what do you call this-- a probe to act when the time will come. Yes. It was a couple times.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people would be killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that time, I couldn't give you [INAUDIBLE]. Only any amount you want-- not by the hundreds, but [? single. ?] Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would a whole family be killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them. By luck, they find a family. Oh, yes. And it was up to them. And I couldn't now judge how many people because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would some people be killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe for no reason. Because they were Jews. And they were on this-- and this life, their life was any value, like a dog. Somebody has a gun and goes by. He wants to kill the dog. He kills the dog. And the same situation with us. All kinds of people and all kinds of times. Was good people, too. Was bad people, like I mentioned to you. I had some people-- maybe I'm not on the subject-- but they gave me life. They gave me life, not God, because they gave me shelter. They gave me help. And at that time, was also not everybody was the killer.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mr. Steinberg, we're at the end of this tape. And we'll resume in just a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Thank you.
